skill.name: xlsx
skill.description: Create, edit, analyze, format, and recalculate spreadsheet files using pandas, openpyxl, and LibreOffice workflows.
skill.summary: Spreadsheet automation guidance for XLSX creation, editing, analysis, formulas, formatting, and recalculation.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most SKILL.md hits are false positives from Markdown examples, Excel cell references, and spreadsheet error names. The confirmed risks are recalc.py executing LibreOffice commands and writing a LibreOffice macro into a hidden user profile directory. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential access, or network exfiltration; publish with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: External LibreOffice Process Execution
medium_findings.description: recalc.py invokes soffice through subprocess.run to recalculate spreadsheets and checks gtimeout on macOS. Arguments are passed as lists rather than through a shell, so command injection evidence is limited, but the skill still executes local external binaries on user-supplied workbooks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run calls are direct and confirmed in recalc.py. The absence of shell=True and use of argument arrays lowers injection confidence, but external process execution is real.

medium_findings.title: LibreOffice Macro Written to Hidden User Profile
medium_findings.description: setup_libreoffice_macro creates or uses a hidden LibreOffice profile directory and writes Module1.xba. This appears intended for formula recalculation, but it modifies user-level application configuration and can persist beyond one workbook operation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly expands a hidden LibreOffice path and writes a macro file there. The context is legitimate recalculation, but the filesystem side effect is confirmed.

low_findings.title: False Positive: XML Namespace URL Is Not Network Access
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is inside the LibreOffice macro XML namespace. No evidence found that recalc.py performs HTTP requests, opens sockets, or sends workbook data to a remote service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 37 is static XML metadata, not executable network code. No network library or request path appears in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most SKILL.md external command and reconnaissance hits are fenced Python, Bash, JSON, inline spreadsheet examples, or code style guidance. They document spreadsheet workflows and do not execute during skill loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are documentation snippets, spreadsheet references, or style guidance. The only operational command they instruct is the expected recalc.py usage.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and C2 hits occur in prose, spreadsheet cell references, or example formulas such as C2. No evidence found of cryptographic APIs, command-and-control logic, or obfuscated traffic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain spreadsheet instructions and examples, not security primitives or network control behavior. This strongly supports a scanner keyword false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: External LibreOffice Process Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: recalc.py invokes soffice through subprocess.run to recalculate spreadsheets and checks gtimeout on macOS. Arguments are passed as lists rather than through a shell, so command injection evidence is limited, but the skill still executes local external binaries on user-supplied workbooks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run calls are direct and confirmed in recalc.py. The absence of shell=True and use of argument arrays lowers injection confidence, but external process execution is real.

dangerous_patterns.title: LibreOffice Macro Written to Hidden User Profile
dangerous_patterns.description: setup_libreoffice_macro creates or uses a hidden LibreOffice profile directory and writes Module1.xba. This appears intended for formula recalculation, but it modifies user-level application configuration and can persist beyond one workbook operation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly expands a hidden LibreOffice path and writes a macro file there. The context is legitimate recalculation, but the filesystem side effect is confirmed.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often fails when formulas, formatting, and recalculation are handled separately. This skill provides practical workflows for creating, editing, analyzing, and validating Excel files.
actual_capabilities: Guides XLSX and CSV reading with pandas for analysis and export tasks.
Guides workbook creation and editing with openpyxl while preserving formulas and formatting.
Defines financial model formatting conventions for inputs, formulas, links, and assumptions.
Provides formula construction rules that keep spreadsheets dynamic and updateable.
Uses recalc.py to trigger LibreOffice recalculation and report formula errors.
Documents checks for common Excel errors such as #REF!, #DIV/0!, and #VALUE!.
limitations: Formula recalculation depends on LibreOffice or compatible command-line tools being installed.
The included script modifies the local LibreOffice profile to install a recalculation macro.
Very large workbooks may take longer to scan because all sheets and cells are checked.
It provides workflow guidance, not a graphical spreadsheet editor.

use_cases.title: Build a financial model
use_cases.description: Create a formatted workbook with assumptions, formulas, source notes, and validation checks.

use_cases.title: Clean and export tabular data
use_cases.description: Read CSV or Excel data, inspect columns, transform values, and export a clean workbook.

use_cases.title: Update an existing workbook
use_cases.description: Modify sheets, rows, columns, and formulas while preserving existing workbook conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an XLSX workbook from this table. Add clear headers, simple formatting, and totals that use Excel formulas.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze spreadsheet data
prompt_templates.prompt: Read this spreadsheet, summarize the columns, identify missing values, and create a cleaned Excel output with the key findings.

prompt_templates.title: Update an existing model
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this workbook with the new assumptions. Preserve existing formatting, add formulas instead of hardcoded calculations, and document changed inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Validate formula quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Recalculate this workbook, inspect every sheet for formula errors, and provide a concise repair plan for each error category found.

output_examples.input: A user provides quarterly sales data and asks for a workbook with totals and growth rates.
output_examples.output: A formatted XLSX workbook with dynamic formulas, labeled assumptions, source notes, and recalculated values.

output_examples.input: A user uploads an existing financial model and asks to add a new projection year.
output_examples.output: An updated workbook that preserves style conventions, extends formulas, and reports whether formula errors remain.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a quick review of messy spreadsheet data.
output_examples.output: A cleaned workbook plus a short summary of missing values, column types, and notable data quality issues.

best_practices: Use Excel formulas for calculated values so workbooks remain dynamic after inputs change.
Recalculate and scan workbooks before delivery when formulas are added or changed.
Preserve established formatting and source documentation when editing existing templates.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode calculated totals, ratios, or averages when a formula can express the logic.
Do not save a workbook opened with data_only=True if formulas must be preserved.
Do not ignore formula error reports after recalculation.

faq.question: Can this skill create new Excel files?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides workflows for creating XLSX files with data, formulas, formatting, and sheet structure.

faq.question: Can it edit existing workbooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes preserving existing formulas, styles, sheet names, and template conventions.

faq.question: How are formulas recalculated?
faq.answer: The included recalc.py script invokes LibreOffice in headless mode and checks the recalculated workbook for Excel errors.

faq.question: Does it send spreadsheet data over the network?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network requests in the reviewed files. The URL detected by static analysis is an XML namespace.

faq.question: What tools does it use?
faq.answer: It documents pandas for data analysis, openpyxl for workbook editing, and LibreOffice for formula recalculation.

faq.question: What security warning should users see?
faq.answer: Users should know that formula recalculation runs local LibreOffice commands and writes a macro into the LibreOffice profile.
